title: Quantitative Stability of Geometric Inequalities: Pr\'ekopa-Leindler and Borell-Brascamp-Lieb
speaker: Peter van Hintum
abstract:
The Prékopa-Leindler inequality (PL) and its strengthening, the Borell-Brascamp-Lieb inequality, are functional extensions of the Brunn-Minkowski inequality from convex geometry, which itself refines the classical isoperimetric inequality. These fundamental inequalities play a central role in geometry, analysis, and probability, motivating the significant attention their stability has garnered in recent years. While significant progress has been made in the geometric setting, a stability results for the Prékopa-Leindler inequality have remained elusive. In this talk, we will explore these inequalities and present recent results that resolve the long-standing question of their quantitative stability.
Based on joint work with Alessio Figalli and Marius Tiba.

proof_stages.label: Set-valued geometric inequalities as volume minimization
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with the isoperimetric inequality and Brunn-Minkowski inequality, interpreting them as statements that Minkowski sums or weighted averages of sets are minimized by convex extremal configurations, then rewrites Brunn-Minkowski in the equal-volume weighted-average form needed for functions.

proof_stages.label: Functional p-means and log-concavity
proof_stages.summary: A family of functional weighted means M_{lambda,p}(f,g) is defined by taking suprema over pairs of points with prescribed weighted average, with the p=0 limit giving geometric means and log-concavity expressed as invariance under self-averaging.

proof_stages.label: Prékopa-Leindler and its Brunn-Minkowski origin
proof_stages.summary: Prékopa-Leindler is stated as the assertion that the integral of the zero-mean interpolation is at least the common integral, and it is related back to Brunn-Minkowski by lifting functions to high-dimensional sets built from small cubes and passing to the limit.

proof_stages.label: Borell-Brascamp-Lieb extension and equality objects
proof_stages.summary: The functional inequality is extended from p=0 to p at least -1/n in the Borell-Brascamp-Lieb theorem, with intuition from box decompositions and equality described by equality of the two functions up to translation together with p-concavity.

proof_stages.label: Stability paradigm for isoperimetry and Brunn-Minkowski
proof_stages.summary: Stability is formulated as the question whether near equality forces near extremizers, and known sharp quantitative stability results for the isoperimetric and Brunn-Minkowski inequalities are reviewed, including convex containment and convex-hull closeness for the summand sets.

proof_stages.label: Functional stability theorem and the failure of containment
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains why Brunn-Minkowski stability cannot be transferred directly to Prékopa-Leindler via dimension lifting and states the functional stability theorem: near equality yields a log-concave, or more generally p-concave, function close to both inputs in integral distance with optimal square-root dependence, with later questions clarifying parameter dependence and unequal integrals.

proof_stages.label: Selecting the log-concave approximant by self-stability
proof_stages.summary: The proof idea is that, instead of taking a naive log-concave hull, one first proves a linear self-averaging stability result and then chooses a minimal log-concave cutoff for which reducing the function causes a proportionally larger reduction in its mean.

examples.name: Indicator functions recover Minkowski averages
examples.note: Taking f and g to be characteristic functions makes the functional mean into the characteristic function of the weighted Minkowski average, showing concretely that the functional definition extends the set-valued one.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker computes the mean of two indicator functions, checking when it is nonzero and identifying it with the indicator of the weighted Minkowski average.

passes.reason: 0–1 indicator functions collapse the functional mean to ordinary set-valued Minkowski averaging.
indicator functions are collapsed 0-1 functions used to recover the set/Minkowski-average case.
0-1 indicator functions collapse the functional mean to the set-valued Minkowski average, used as a reduction rather than an obstruction.
Indicator functions are binary, collapsed functional representatives of sets, used to show the functional mean reduces to a weighted Minkowski average.
Characteristic functions are a collapsed two-valued special case of the functional mean, used to recover the set Minkowski average.
Indicator functions are collapsed 0-1 functions used to show the functional mean reduces to Minkowski averaging.
Characteristic functions are a collapsed 0-1 functional case used to recover the set-valued Minkowski average, not to refute a claim.
Indicator functions are a collapsed 0/1 special case of nonnegative functions used to recover the set-valued Minkowski average, not to refute a claim.
0-1 indicator functions are a collapsed set-valued subcase used to show the functional mean recovers Minkowski averages.
Binary indicator functions are used illustratively to recover the set-valued Minkowski average as a collapsed special case of the functional mean.
Indicator functions are a collapsed 0/1 functional special case used to recover the set-valued Minkowski average, not to refute a claim.
Indicator functions are a collapsed 0-1 set-valued subclass of nonnegative functions used to recover Minkowski averages.

examples.name: Marginal of a log-concave function
examples.note: A log-concave function on R^2 is integrated along one coordinate to illustrate how Prékopa-Leindler proves that the resulting marginal remains log-concave.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker takes a log-concave function on R^2, integrates along lines, and reasons via Prékopa-Leindler that the marginal is log-concave.

passes.reason: A generic marginal of a log-concave function is used as a standard application of Prékopa-Leindler.
a marginal of a log-concave function is a standard systematic construction illustrating Prékopa-Leindler, not a special or adversarial object.
a generic log-concave function is integrated along one coordinate to illustrate that marginals remain log-concave.

examples.name: Perturbed rectangles for sharp sqrt-delta dependence
examples.note: Two rectangles differing by a small root-delta deformation are used to demonstrate that the square-root dependence in the stability estimates is optimal, and the same construction is later reused through indicator functions for the functional theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker cites slightly perturbed rectangles as the sharpness example for the sqrt(delta) dependence but does not carry out the rectangle computation in the transcript.

passes.reason: The perturbed rectangles are a tight sharpness example realizing the optimal square-root dependence.
the perturbed rectangles are near-extremizers making the sqrt-delta stability exponent tight and ruling out better dependence.
near-equality perturbed rectangles are constructed to saturate the sharp sqrt-delta stability rate.
The slightly perturbed rectangles realize the optimal sqrt-delta scaling, so they are a tight sharpness example rather than a counterexample.
The perturbed rectangles are chosen to realize the optimal sharp sqrt-delta scaling in the stability bound.
The slightly varied rectangles are sharpness examples saturating the optimal sqrt-delta stability dependence.
The perturbed rectangles form a sharpness family attaining the optimal square-root dependence, rather than serving as a refutation of the stated theorem.
Slightly perturbed rectangles are near-extremizers showing the sqrt-delta stability exponent is tight, rather than serving as a counterexample in the stated role.
The slightly perturbed rectangles are used as a sharpness construction realizing the tight sqrt-delta stability rate.
Slightly perturbed rectangles are used as sharpness examples attaining the optimal square-root dependence in the stability estimate.
The slightly perturbed rectangles are a sharpness/tightness construction for the optimal sqrt-delta stability rate, rather than a refutation of the stated theorem.
The perturbed rectangles form a near-equality family attaining the optimal sqrt-delta stability rate.

examples.name: Interval with a tiny remote spike
examples.note: A one-dimensional indicator-like function with a small far-away peak shows why containment in a log-concave envelope is the wrong stability notion and motivates measuring closeness in integral/symmetric-difference form and later the cutoff criterion for choosing the approximant.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes an interval with a tiny far-away spike, tracing how its log-concave majorant and self-mean behave and what is removed by capping.

passes.reason: The tiny far-away spike is a vanishing outlier used to show containment by a log-concave majorant can fail.
the tiny remote spike is a vanishing, at-infinity limiting perturbation used to obstruct any log-concave containment statement.
an interval indicator with an arbitrarily tiny distant spike is a limiting degenerate perturbation used to obstruct any log-concave-majorant containment statement.
